text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 09 February 2022

Dr Amelia Grant
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Amelia Grant,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with recent deterioration in seizure control; history of traumatic brain injury; persistent cognitive difficulties; osteoporosis; previous alcohol dependence in sustained remission; currently residing in supervised accommodation; active smoker.

Medication: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; Carbamazepine MR 400 mg twice daily; Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening; Buccolam (rescue) as needed; Vagus Nerve Stimulator (VNS) in situ; plus Vitamin B compound, Calcium/Vitamin D supplements, Thiamine, Atorvastatin, Lansoprazole, Bisoprolol, Ramipril, Carbocisteine, emollients.

I reviewed Bill in clinic today, accompanied by his workplace liaison and residential keyworker. Since his last review, there has been a notable deterioration in seizure control and overall stability. He is employed part‑time in a cleanroom/data‑hall environment with strict access controls. He and his supervisors report that retrieving rescue medication and obtaining timely refills is often significantly delayed due to site security procedures, resulting in missed doses and delayed administration. These constraints appear temporally associated with more frequent breakthrough events and slower post‑ictal recovery.

Seizures: He carries a diagnosis of generalised epilepsy. His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced five days apart, though brief periods of daily seizures have been reported. VNS is used more frequently, and Buccolam was administered once each month over the past 3 months. Compared with his previous pattern of infrequent partial events, this represents a clear worsening. Post‑ictally he is increasingly confused for up to 30–60 minutes and has required workplace observation on multiple occasions. No clear new triggers have been identified apart from the access‑related delays in medication availability.

Examination: He was alert and cooperative, mildly tremulous post‑ictally by report but settled in clinic. Gait cautious but independent. Speech slow with word‑finding pauses consistent with his baseline cognitive difficulties. No focal neurological deficits elicited. VNS site intact without signs of infection.

Investigations/Monitoring: No recent drug levels available. Residential staff report occasional missed evening doses when returning late from work due to pharmacy collection delays. Seizure diary entries from the keyworker corroborate an increased clustering pattern over the last eight weeks.

Plan: In view of the deterioration in a patient with generalised epilepsy, we discussed adherence support and timely access to medications at his workplace. We will coordinate with occupational health and his residential team to ensure on‑site availability of rescue medication and streamlined refill arrangements through a pre‑authorised delivery schedule. We will request updated bloods including FBC, U&E, LFTs, vitamin D, and consider trough levetiracetam and topiramate levels where feasible. Device interrogation of the VNS will be arranged to review usage and consider parameter adjustment. We encouraged continuation of the seizure diary and prompt reporting of any further daily clustering. Smoking cessation advice was reiterated given potential impact on general health and bone density.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced five days apart

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Step 1: Identify explicit frequency statements in the letter. The letter states: (a) "His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced five days apart" and (b) "brief periods of daily seizures have been reported." It also notes rescue medication use once monthly, which reflects rescue use rather than baseline seizure frequency. Step 2: Determine the prevailing pattern. The wording "typically" and "generally spaced five days apart" describes the dominant ongoing pattern, and the diary corroborates an increased clustering pattern over the last eight weeks. The "brief periods of daily seizures" are described as transient, not the sustained pattern. Step 3: Normalize to an allowed format. The cluster spacing gives a clear numeric interval (five days). As the number of seizures per cluster is not specified, a cluster-based format with per-cluster count cannot be completed; therefore, normalize the dominant interval to a per-time frequency. Step 4: Finalize the single frequency label as 1 per 5 day, reflecting the typical spacing described.
seizure_frequency_number: His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced five days apart
reference: His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced five days apart, though brief periods of daily seizures have been reported. VNS is used more frequently, and Buccolam was administered once each month over the past 3 months
